title: Nathanial Breaks Through in Bahrain Thriller as No One Retires — Until the Final Metre
meta: Published: 13 April 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Joshua Nathanial crosses the line to win his first grand prix of 2026 — a perfectly timed drive after a nightmare start to the season.
body:
Joshua Nathanial won the Bahrain Grand Prix after a race of relentless pressure, strategic cunning, and a final lap that saw the McLaren driver finally shake off the ghosts of three non‑finishes to stand on the top step for the first time in 2026. Behind him, Eren Aygen and Jack Tef completed a remarkable Williams two‑three, the team principal stepping into the cockpit in place of the injured Lucas Koopman and delivering a podium on his racing return. Jason Roy took fourth after a late charge on fresh tyres, while Michiil MaraDöner claimed fifth in a race that ran its full 57 laps with no retirements — until George Konstantaras ran out of fuel within sight of the finish line.

The race began under clear desert skies, a complete contrast to the rain‑soaked qualifying that had scrambled the grid. Lei Zhao started from pole, but his advantage evaporated within four laps. Quarter Kevu, on the medium compound, challenged the McLaren immediately, while Richard Hicks, starting an outstanding fourth for Audi, seized the lead by lap three. The Audi’s recent form — a breakthrough win in Japan — had carried into Bahrain, and Hicks’s early pace suggested another strong result. But the race was only just beginning to unfold its strategic layers.

By lap five, the top four — Hicks, Kevu, Jason Roy, and MaraDöner — were running in close company, with DRS enabled and the soft‑tyre runners already feeling the strain. Kevu passed Hicks for the lead on lap eight, only for Roy to follow him through a lap later. The battle between the ex‑teammates from Racing Bulls and Red Bull was fierce, the two touching at one point as Kevu defended the inside line and Roy tried to squeeze through. Kevu held on, but Roy’s persistence was rewarded on lap nine when he retook the lead, a move that sparked a prolonged duel at the front.

The first pit‑stop window, triggered by the soft‑compound degradation, opened around lap 13. Philippe Verhaeren was the first to blink, diving into the pits early. The leaders — Kevu and Roy — stopped a lap later, both switching to another set of softs. The undercut played its usual tricks: Kevu emerged ahead, but Roy was held up by other cars, dropping him behind the Alpines and into a chasing position. When the medium runners — including Joshua Nathanial, who had started on the harder compound — finally stopped, Nathanial emerged in the lead, his long first stint vaulting him to the front of the pack. It was a position he would not surrender.

Behind Nathanial, the race fractured into a series of intense micro‑battles. Eren Aygen and Jack Tef, the Williams pair, were locked in a tense intra‑team tussle for second and third, their positions swapping as the laps ticked down. On lap 52, Aygen passed Tef for second, a move that Tef — the team principal, filling in for the injured Koopman — could not answer. Aygen would hold that position to the flag, delivering Williams their best result of the season with a two‑three finish that catapulted them to the top of the constructors’ championship.

Further back, the midfield was a maelstrom. MaraDöner, after a difficult middle stint, fought back past both McLarens to secure fifth, while Xiaolay Zhao — the pole‑sitter — could only manage sixth after struggling with tyre wear. Quarter Kevu faded to seventh, his medium‑soft strategy proving a lap too long. Wesley Vandesteene took eighth for Mercedes, and Riley Swansson, on his Ferrari debut following the Cachinho swap, scored points in ninth, a result that will ease the immediate pressure on the second scarlet cockpit. Philippe Verhaeren rounded out the top ten.

The race was notable for what did not happen: no retirements until the very end. On the final lap, George Konstantaras’s car spluttered to a halt out of fuel, the Haas coasting to a stop just metres from the line. He was classified 20th, a cruel end to a weekend that had already seen him qualify 19th. But for the rest of the field, the chequered flag fell without a single crash, a rarity in modern Formula 1 that spoke to the discipline of the grid.

Nathanial’s victory rewrites his season. Entering Bahrain with zero points and three DNFs, he leaves as a grand prix winner, his ninth place in the championship belied by the raw speed he displayed. Williams’s double podium, meanwhile, shakes up the constructors’ title race: they now lead with 99 points, Ferrari are second on 66, and McLaren have vaulted to third. Audi, after their early‑season surge, slip to fourth. The 2026 campaign, so recently dominated by Ferrari, has been thrown wide open. The paddock now heads to Jeddah, where the high‑speed challenge of the Corniche circuit will pose an entirely new set of questions. For now, though, the story is Nathanial’s — a driver who refused to let his season end before it began.
